findings.title: AuditService.log passes undefined-valued payload keys to computeRowHash; stored JSONB normalizes them differently, risking hash divergence on external recompute
findings.code_evidence:
payload: {
          ip: event.ip,
          details: event.details,
          success: event.success,
          timestamp: entry.timestamp,
          ...(event.ua !== undefined ? { ua: event.ua } : {}),
          ...(event.reason !== undefined ? { reason: event.reason } : {}),
          ...(event.before !== undefined ? { before: event.before } : {}),
          ...(event.after !== undefined ? { after: event.after } : {}),
        },
findings.description:
AuditService.log always includes `ip: event.ip` and `details: event.details` in the payload object, even when those values are `undefined`. The payload is then passed to AuditPersistenceService.log, which calls computeRowHash → stableStringify(payload). In stableStringify, Object.keys() includes keys with undefined values, and stableStringify(undefined) returns 'null'. So the hash is computed over a payload like {"details":null,"ip":null,"success":true,"timestamp":"..."}.

However, when this payload is stored in PostgreSQL JSONB via Prisma, undefined-valued keys may be dropped (analogous to JSON.stringify behavior), so the stored JSONB becomes {"success":true,"timestamp":"..."}. When an external recompute reads the stored row and calls computeRowHash(storedRow), stableStringify produces {"success":true,"timestamp":"..."}, which differs from the original canonical string. The stored rowHash cannot be reproduced from the stored data.

Note: this divergence only materializes if Prisma drops undefined-valued keys during JSONB persistence. If Prisma stores them as JSON null, stableStringify reads back null-valued keys and the hashes agree. The Prisma behavior on undefined JSONB keys is the load-bearing assumption; confidence is medium until verified (see verification_command). The contracts other emitters (emitDataExported, emitSystemBackfill) consistently guard optionals with !== undefined ? {k:v} : {}, but the security envelope path does not — that contrast is supporting evidence.

Secondary vector (same root cause, assumption-free): the payload-schemas.ts comment states snapshots carry 'Dates, Prisma.Decimal, nested rows' (z.unknown() fields). stableStringify(new Date()) returns '{}' (no enumerable own keys on a Date), but PostgreSQL JSONB stores a Date as an ISO string. On read-back the stored form is a string, and stableStringify('2026-...')  returns '"2026-..."'. These are different canonical forms for the same logical value, breaking recompute for any row whose before/after/snapshot contained a JS Date object.
findings.root_cause: computeRowHash canonicalizes the in-memory JS object (where undefined stays and Date has no enumerable keys) while PostgreSQL JSONB normalizes the stored form (undefined dropped, Date coerced to ISO string), so the hash input at write time and the hash input at re-read time can diverge.
findings.impact: An external or maintenance recompute of the audit hash chain (e.g. recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts run after a new schema bump, or an independent auditor verifying the chain) would find that rowHash values for rows written via AuditService.log do not match the hash recomputed from stored fields. This defeats the tamper-detection guarantee that computeRowHash's own doc advertises: 'Exported so an external verifier (production runbook, chain-audit script) can recompute and compare against stored values.' No live functionality is broken — the audit module is write-only in production and the recompute scripts self-heal by overwriting — but the integrity guarantee is latent-broken for affected rows.
findings.suggested_fix: Strip undefined-valued keys and normalise non-serializable types before calling computeRowHash. One approach: after the payload is assembled in AuditService.log, round-trip it through JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(payload)) to produce a JSONB-canonical plain object (drops undefined, serialises Date → ISO string, Prisma.Decimal → string). Pass this normalised form to both computeRowHash and the DB insert. Alternatively, guard ip/details with the same !== undefined pattern already used for ua/reason/before/after.
acceptance_criteria: When AuditService.log is called without ip/details (both undefined), the stored payload in audit_logs contains no 'ip' or 'details' keys, and computeRowHash(storedRow) === storedRow.rowHash.
When a before/after value contains a Date object, computeRowHash(storedRow) === storedRow.rowHash after the row is read back from PostgreSQL.
A chain verify pass over rows written via AuditService.log finds 0 rowHash mismatches.
findings.verification_command: pnpm test:integration -- --testPathPattern audit-persistence; also: insert a row via AuditService.log with no ip/details, then run: docker exec orchestr-a-db psql -U postgres orchestra -c "SELECT payload, \"rowHash\" FROM audit_logs ORDER BY \"createdAt\" DESC LIMIT 1" and manually call computeRowHash with the stored fields to compare.
findings.notes: The critical unknown is Prisma's JSONB serialization of undefined-valued object keys. If Prisma stores them as JSON null (not drops them), the hashes agree and this is a non-issue. The Date vector (secondary) is assumption-free: Date objects have no enumerable own properties, so stableStringify(date) = '{}', but stored JSONB would be an ISO string.

verified_clean: health/health.service.ts: DB ping error path (catch block logs, sets dbStatus='down') — correct, no swallowed exception
health/health.service.ts: Redis ping error path (catch block logs, sets redisStatus='down') — correct, no swallowed exception
health/health.service.ts: check() throws ServiceUnavailableException when either dep is down — correct status code mapping
health/health.service.ts: Redis lazyConnect + enableOfflineQueue=false — correct fail-fast health check semantics
deployments/deployments.service.ts: readAppliedMigrations catch returns [] — intentional best-effort, does not block boot
deployments/deployments.service.ts: onApplicationBootstrap wraps recordBoot in try/catch — correctly prevents startup failure
deployments/deployments.service.ts: isDeployContext uses NODE_ENV=production OR RELEASE_SHA present — correct dual guard
deployments/deployments.service.ts: audit.log is fire-and-forget (AuditService.log is synchronous void) — intentional, documented
testing/testing.controller.ts: production guard at handler level as defense-in-depth in addition to conditional module registration — correct
audit/audit.service.ts: resolveEntityId correctly routes LOGIN_FAILURE/ACCOUNT_LOCKED to HMAC, others to targetId/userId/'unknown' — correct
audit/audit.service.ts: hashAttemptedSubject throws if AUDIT_HASH_KEY missing — correct defence-in-depth, reject-not-fallback pattern
audit/audit.service.ts: bcrypt-shape guard returns 'unknown' before hashing — correct credential-in-log prevention
audit/audit-persistence.service.ts: pg_advisory_xact_lock serialises all inserts — correct total-order guarantee for hash chain
audit/audit-persistence.service.ts: prevHash read uses ORDER BY createdAt DESC, id DESC — consistent with recompute ASC ordering for same-timestamp rows
audit/audit-persistence.service.ts: createdAt set before transaction open but included in hash — intentional; both stored and hashed values are identical at write time
audit/audit-persistence.service.ts: DAT-006 outerTx path acquires advisory lock on caller's tx — correctly scoped to outer transaction commit
audit/audit-persistence.service.ts: validatePayloadForAction called BEFORE transaction opens — correctly rejects malformed payloads without holding the advisory lock
audit/recompute-chain.ts: predecessor-untouched assertion prevents off-by-one rewriting sealed prefix rows — correct safety guard
audit/recompute-chain.ts: in-transaction verification re-reads and re-hashes before commit — correct catch of walk logic errors
audit/recompute-chain.ts: tuple comparison (createdAt, id) ordering consistent between write (DESC) and recompute (ASC) for same-timestamp tiebreak — verified consistent
audit/payload-schemas.ts: AUDIT_PAYLOAD_SCHEMAS satisfies Record<AuditAction, ZodTypeAny> — exhaustiveness enforced at compile time via compile-witness
audit/payload-schemas.ts: .strict() rejects unknown top-level keys — correct gate for payload predictability
audit/payload-schemas.ts: LEAVE_APPROVED union(leaveAudit, securityEnvelope) — correctly handles dual-provenance writes
scripts/normalize-action-codes.ts: STARTED emitted before transaction, COMPLETED after — ordering correct; STARTED row re-hashed by the recompute walk
scripts/normalize-action-codes.ts: DryRunRollback throws inside transaction — trigger DISABLE rolled back automatically, no stuck-disabled state
scripts/normalize-action-codes.ts: DATABASE_MIGRATION_URL guard fails fast before Nest boot — correct privilege enforcement
scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts: full-chain recompute from genesis anchors on null predecessor — correctly handles empty-chain case
scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts: idempotency correct — second run recomputes identical hashes, verification still passes
scripts/system-backfill-audit.ts: emitSystemBackfill awaits (not fire-and-forget) for durable maintenance event — correct
scripts/backfill-snapshots.ts: app.close() in finally block — correct cleanup even on error
scripts/normalize-action-codes.ts: app.close() in finally block — correct cleanup
scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts: app.close() in finally block — correct cleanup
schema-constraints/: all files are integration spec files exercising DB-level constraints; no source logic to audit for correctness bugs
UUID chain tiebreak: UUID (random) tiebreak does not break chain consistency — write path reads DESC, recompute walks ASC; both orderings are symmetric for same-timestamp pairs
Numeric precision: MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (9007199254740991) in witness payload is exactly representable as IEEE-754 double; no precision loss on JSONB round-trip
deployments dual-write non-atomicity: intentional by design — audit row is best-effort, deployments row is source of truth
stableStringify undefined at top-level: if value === undefined returns 'null' — only affects in-memory representation before DB storage, not an issue for keys passed through Object.keys
